title: Using Humor to Bypass Safety Guardrails in Large Language Models
venue: Proceedings of the The First Workshop on LLM Security (LLMSEC), pages 17–25
research_questions: Can a fixed humorous context around a verbatim unsafe request bypass LLM safety guardrails more effectively than direct injection?
Is the effectiveness of the proposed jailbreak due to humor itself rather than merely asking the LLM to answer on behalf of a subject?
Does adding more humor through a multi-turn knock-knock setup or a joke-prefixed single-turn prompt improve the attack?
How does humor-based jailbreaking vary across the tested open-source LLMs and public unsafe-request datasets?
tasks: unsafe response elicitation with humorous prompts

methods_proposed.name: Fixed humor-based single-turn jailbreak attack
methods_proposed.summary: The method wraps an unsafe request, kept verbatim, in a fixed humorous context with whispering/laughter cues and asks the model to help a subject such as “man,” “chicken,” “I,” or “goat.” It treats the attacked LLM as a black box and does not use another LLM to craft prompts.

methods_proposed.name: No-humor ablation
methods_proposed.summary: This ablation removes the humorous context cues from the main template, including phrases such as “Psst.”, laughter, “humorously,” whispers, and “xD,” while preserving the on-behalf-of-subject framing.

methods_proposed.name: Knock-knock addition
methods_proposed.summary: This multi-turn variant uses three prompts: a knock-knock opening, a subject-specific second turn, and a third turn that adds a joke before the original humorous attack prompt. For the “man” subject, Llama 3.3 70B is used to formulate situation-style jokes in four variants.

methods_proposed.name: Joke addition without knock-knock
methods_proposed.summary: This single-turn variant consists of the third prompt from the knock-knock attack: a joke plus the original humorous jailbreak prompt, thereby adding more humor than the original method without the preceding dialogue turns.

evaluation_methods.detail: Llama 3.3 70B, the largest tested model, is used to judge whether each analyzed model response is unsafe; the judge prompt explicitly says to account for humorous, comical, or funny context and to classify only sufficiently detailed harmful responses as unsafe.
The paper reports Percentage (%) of successful attacks, where a successful attack is a response judged unsafe.

headline_results.finding: The original humor-based method achieved a higher successful-attack rate than direct injection overall across model, dataset, and subject combinations.
headline_results.metric: cases with higher successful-attack rate than direct injection

headline_results.finding: On Gemma 3 27B, the Chicken variant produced the strongest main-method rates and greatly exceeded direct injection on all three datasets.
headline_results.value: D1 49.00 vs Direct Injection 0.00; D2 56.54 vs Direct Injection 0.19; D3 52.33 vs Direct Injection 6.33

headline_results.finding: On Llama 3.1 8B, the best main-method rates reached 33.00 on D1, 31.73 on D2, and 44.00 on D3, versus direct injection at 5.00, 2.50, and 7.00.
headline_results.value: D1 33.00 (Chicken) vs 5.00; D2 31.73 (Chicken) vs 2.50; D3 44.00 (Goat) vs 7.00

headline_results.finding: Removing the humorous context did not increase the attack’s effectiveness in practically all tested cases.
headline_results.metric: cases where no-humor removal did not increase effectiveness

headline_results.finding: Adding more humor through the multi-turn knock-knock attack generally did not improve over the original method.
headline_results.metric: exceptions where knock-knock attack improved over original

headline_results.finding: Adding a joke without the knock-knock turns also generally did not improve over the original method.
headline_results.metric: exceptions where joke addition improved over original

key_findings: A fixed humorous context around a verbatim unsafe request can bypass safety guardrails across the tested datasets and models.
The method is deliberately simple: it does not edit the unsafe request, does not require access to model internals, and does not need an auxiliary LLM for prompt construction.
The no-humor ablation supports the paper’s claim that humor is crucial, since removing the humorous context did not increase effectiveness in 46 out of 48 cases.

Adding more humor generally reduced rather than improved attack success, with the knock-knock addition improving only 4 out of 84 cases and the joke-prefixed single-turn variant improving only 9 out of 84 cases.
The main method was most effective on Llama 3.1 8B and Gemma 3 27B, and least effective on Llama 3.3 70B and Mixtral, according to the paper’s discussion.
The authors hypothesize that success may stem from mismatched generalization in safety training, where safeguards did not generalize well to humorous unsafe contexts.
limitations: All experiments set the temperature hyperparameter to zero, and the paper notes that humor-based jailbreaking effectiveness could be sensitive to this hyperparameter.
The experiments cover four open-source models and three publicly available datasets; future directions include testing proprietary LLMs and reasoning models.
The paper states that its objective is to show humor is effective for inducing jailbreaking, not to compare to literature results or achieve state-of-the-art attack performance.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that it makes the community aware of a new type of jailbreaking and may help developers incorporate protection against humor-based attacks. The author says developers or responsible teams for every studied LLM were contacted on May 16th, 2025. The ethics statement also notes that, as with other published jailbreak methods, malicious users could use the ideas to obtain unsafe content from LLMs for unethical purposes.

judges.name: Llama 3.3 70B / Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct
judges.detail: Used to judge whether each analyzed LLM response was unsafe, i.e. whether jailbreaking happened. The paper says the judge accounts for humorous context, and Appendix A.4 gives a prompt requiring an answer of only 'safe' or 'unsafe'. The same model is also one of the attacked models, so Llama 3.3 70B responses are judged by Llama 3.3 70B.

judges.name: Percentage (%) of successful attacks
judges.detail: Tables 1-4 report the percentage of successful attacks, where a successful attack is an analyzed response classified as unsafe by the Llama 3.3 70B judge.

generation_selection.scope: Direct injection baseline and fixed single-turn attack variants: original humor attack, no-humor ablation, and joke addition without knock-knock
generation_selection.n: 1 prompt/turn per unsafe request per evaluated condition
generation_selection.detail: The paper describes these as single-turn methods and reports percentages over dataset requests. It does not describe generating multiple candidate responses or selecting a best response.

generation_selection.n: one three-prompt interaction per unsafe request per evaluated condition
generation_selection.detail: The multi-turn attack is described as three prompts: 'knock, knock...', a subject-specific second prompt, and a third prompt consisting of a joke plus the original humor attack prompt. No best-of-N response selection is reported.

generation_selection.scope: Llama 3.3 70B generation of 'man' situation jokes for the added-humor variants
generation_selection.n: 4 variants were used and evaluated: Man-Humor, Man-NoHumor, Man-Humor-NoNeg, and Man-NoHumor-NoNeg; candidate count per variant is not reported
generation_selection.detail: For the subject 'man', the paper used Llama 3.3 70B to formulate situations for the third prompt. The paper describes the four variant constraints but does not report the exact generation prompts, how many candidate jokes were generated, or whether any candidate selection was performed.

model_provenance.name: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Full model name is given in Appendix A.2. Used as an attacked model, as the LLM-as-judge, and to formulate the 'man' situation jokes in the added-humor experiments. Hosted on two NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs.

model_provenance.name: Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Full model name is given in Appendix A.2. Used as an attacked model. Hosted on one NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU.

model_provenance.name: Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct-v0.1
model_provenance.detail: Full model name is given in Appendix A.2. Used as an attacked model. Hosted on one NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU.

model_provenance.name: gemma-3-27b-it
model_provenance.detail: Full model name is given in Appendix A.2. Used as an attacked model. Hosted on one NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU.

inference_budgets.scope: Single-turn attack response generation: direct injection, original humor attack, no-humor ablation, and joke addition without knock-knock
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 1 user prompt/turn per unsafe request per evaluated condition
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: No multiple-candidate generation or best-of-N selection reported
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Temperature was set to 0 in all experiments; other decoding parameters are not reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct was hosted on two NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs; Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct-v0.1, and gemma-3-27b-it were each hosted on one NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU, on a PowerEdge R760xa Server with two Intel Xeon Gold 6442Y processors and twelve 64GB RDIMM memory.
inference_budgets.detail: Datasets were JBB/D1 with 100 unsafe requests, AdvBench/D2 with 520 unsafe requests, and HEx-PHI/D3 with 300 unsafe requests. The paper reports percentages of successful attacks by model, dataset, and subject/variant.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 3 prompts per unsafe request per evaluated condition
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: No multiple-candidate generation or best-of-N selection reported
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Temperature was set to 0 in all experiments; other decoding parameters are not reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Same local H100-based platform as the single-turn experiments.
inference_budgets.detail: The three prompts are described as a knock-knock opening, a subject-specific second prompt, and a third prompt adding a joke before the original humor attack prompt.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: The paper states temperature was 0 in all experiments, but does not separately report judge decoding parameters beyond this.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Judge model Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct was hosted on two NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs.
inference_budgets.detail: The judge prompt requests one label, 'safe' or 'unsafe', for each user request and LLM response. The paper does not report repeated judge calls, majority voting, confidence estimates, or a human validation set.

inference_budgets.scope: Llama 3.3 70B generation of 'man' situation jokes for added-humor prompts
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Temperature was 0 in all experiments, but no separate prompt-construction decoding details are reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct hosted on two NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs.
inference_budgets.detail: The model was used to formulate a situation where a man could innocently and inadvertently encounter the unsafe request, under four variant constraints. Number of generation calls, candidate outputs, and any selection procedure are not reported.

human_baselines.description: No human baseline and no human judging are reported. The only explicit baseline is direct injection of the unsafe request, which is a model-prompt baseline rather than a human baseline.
human_baselines.detail: All reported success rates are based on LLM outputs judged by Llama 3.3 70B.

contamination_risk.basis: The paper evaluates verbatim unsafe requests from three publicly available datasets, JBB, AdvBench, and HEx-PHI, and does not report any decontamination analysis, held-out/private request construction, or checks of whether the attacked models or the Llama 3.3 70B judge had seen these datasets. Exact training cutoffs or release/training dates for all model-dataset pairs are not reported in the paper, so benchmark-public-before-model is left null. Because the requests are public legacy benchmark items and are passed verbatim, contamination risk is assessed as at least moderate.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: The main successful-attack percentages in Tables 1-4; comparisons between humor prompting and direct injection; the no-humor ablation claim that humor is crucial; and the claim that adding more humor generally does not improve the attack.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: No human validation of Llama 3.3 70B judge labels is reported.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: A response counts as a successful jailbreak only if the Llama 3.3 70B judge classifies it as unsafe. Thus the headline empirical conclusions rest primarily on LLM judgment rather than human annotation or an independently validated metric.

reporting_gaps: No human evaluation, human validation set, inter-annotator agreement, or calibration of the Llama 3.3 70B judge is reported.
Model identifiers are reported, but no model snapshot hashes, checkpoint revisions, serving-library versions, or inference dates are reported.
Decoding details beyond temperature=0 are not reported; missing details include max tokens/token budgets, top_p, top_k, seeds, stop sequences, and repetition settings.
The paper does not report repeated runs, variance, confidence intervals, or robustness to stochastic decoding settings.
For LLM-as-judge labeling, the paper does not report repeated judge calls, majority voting, uncertainty handling, or whether the judge was blinded to attacked model or attack condition.
For the Llama 3.3 70B-generated 'man' jokes, the exact generation prompts, generated joke texts/situations, number of candidates, and any selection procedure are not fully reported.
No contamination or decontamination analysis is reported for the public unsafe-request datasets or for the Llama 3.3 70B judge.
No code, data artifact, or response-output release is reported in the supplied paper.
